ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for deep-fried beef cubes with mala sauce; 'beef dish' (FOODON_03302757) used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef meat, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for deep-fried beef preparation; mala sauce component not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of deep fried beef cubes with mala spicy sauce weighs approximately 350 g, typically shared as part of a Zi Char meal or consumed as a single main dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, composite of trimmed retail cuts, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 fat, all grades, cooked (SR Legacy ID 13365) combined with estimated deep-frying oil absorption and mala sauce component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for deep-fried beef cubes with mala sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked beef (ID 13365) with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption (~8–10% fat increase per standard frying models) and mala sauce sodium contribution estimated from USDA soy sauce and chilli paste entries. The input record values (energy 242.86 kcal, protein 12.86 g, fat 18.57 g, carbohydrate 7.14 g, sodium 514.29 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this adapted model and are retained as the best available estimate. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on mixed meat dish with cornstarch coating and sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried beef cubes with mala sauce. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low category) based on analogous mixed meat dishes with starch coatings from the University of Sydney GI Database. The primary carbohydrate source is cornstarch coating (GI ~68 for pure cornstarch) and mala sauce (minimal carbohydrate contribution). The high protein and fat content of the dish significantly blunts the glycaemic response, supporting a composite dish GI estimate in the low range (~40–50). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (7.14 g/100g × 350 g = 24.99 g) / 100 = 11.25; however, effective available carbohydrate after fibre deduction is approximately 5.0 g per 100 g, giving GL per serving = 45 × (5.0 × 3.5) / 100 = 7.88 ≈ 7.9. Simplified GL reported as 2.25 per 100 g equivalent portion for comparability.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 514 mg per 100 g from mala sauce, broad bean paste, and soy sauce, with minimal potassium or dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its sodium content of 514 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,800 mg per standard 350 g plate), which approaches or exceeds the daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board; request reduced sauce or substitute with a lower-sodium preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate load (7.14 g per 100 g) with high protein moderating glycaemic response, but high fat content and deep-frying increase caloric density and may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycaemic index (~45) and modest carbohydrate content, making single moderate portions acceptable for most individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat content (18.57 g per 100 g) and caloric density (242.86 kcal per 100 g) require strict portion control to avoid weight gain and progressive insulin resistance — limit to no more than 150–200 g per meal occasion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (5.71 g per 100 g), trans fat (0.14 g per 100 g), high sodium (514 mg per 100 g), and deep-frying-associated advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) collectively increase cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish; the combination of saturated fat at 5.71 g per 100 g (exceeding the 5 g threshold for high concern), trans fat, high sodium, and AGEs from deep-frying creates a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk profile that is not offset by the modest protein content — if consumed, limit to an occasional small portion and pair with high-fibre, low-sodium accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; beef is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6); chilli in mala sauce is a nightshade ingredient; saturated fat exceeds 5 g per 100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to the combination of deep-frying-generated AGEs, high arachidonic acid from beef, nightshade ingredients (chilli, dried chillies) in mala sauce, and saturated fat at 5.71 g per 100 g — all of which independently and synergistically amplify inflammatory pathways associated with RA flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep Fried Beef Cube with Mala Spicy Sauce (麻辣炸牛肉块) is a popular Singaporean Zi Char dish with Malaysian-Chinese culinary influences. Tender beef cubes are marinated, coated in a light cornstarch batter, and deep-fried until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains succulent and juicy. The fried cubes are then tossed in a fiery mala sauce made from Sichuan peppercorns, dried chillies, broad bean paste (doubanjiang), soy sauce, garlic, and ginger, producing a characteristic numbing (má) and spicy (là) flavour profile. The dish delivers approximately 243 kcal per 100 g, with 12.9 g protein, 18.6 g fat (5.7 g saturated), and 7.1 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 514 mg per 100 g, primarily from the mala sauce components. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), making the carbohydrate load modest despite the cornstarch coating. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, trans fat, and AGE content from deep-frying. It is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate load but high caloric density. It is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. A standard plate serving is approximately 350 g. This dish is commonly found at Zi Char stalls and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia, and is typically ordered as a shared main dish in a group meal setting.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for deep-fried beef cubes with mala sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked beef with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption and mala sauce sodium contribution. The input record values are consistent with this adapted model and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this composite deep-fried beef and mala sauce dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16; only total fat and saturated fat are reported. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation not available in approved sources. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish not reported in approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this specific composite dish preparation not available in approved sources as of 2026-04-16; USDA SR Legacy cooked beef entries provide some of these values for raw beef but not for the composite deep-fried mala preparation. Halal status is marked false as mala sauce preparations may contain non-halal ingredients (e.g., lard-based chilli oil); verify with specific vendor. Trans fat at 0.14 g per 100 g is a low but non-zero value consistent with deep-frying in partially hydrogenated oils or from natural ruminant trans fats in beef. Glycemic load per serving (350 g plate) calculated as: GI 45 × net carbohydrate (7.14 − 0.86 = 6.28 g per 100 g × 3.5) / 100 = 45 × 21.98 / 100 = 9.89; reported gi_category as low. The gi_source field documents the analogous estimation basis per Q4 requirements.
